projects.name: Solar + Energy Storage on Garage
projects.description: Install 5-7 solar panels on south-facing garage roof face (20x12 ft, ~2-2.8 kW) plus battery storage. Solar = standalone ROI investment (~5yr payback at $0.42/kWh). Battery = generator alternative (no lot space for generator). 30% federal ITC applies to both when paired.
projects.notes: DESIGN: South garage face only. 5 portrait panels = 2.0 kW; 7 mixed = 2.8 kW. ~3,200-4,500 kWh/yr production (20-26% offset). All-in after ITC: solar ~$9-10k, battery ~$9-10k. VENDORS: Sunergy (sunergy.com) — EnergySage 2026 CA Installer of Year, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door. NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com) — Diamond Certified, 35yr, San Mateo County, 40yr warranty. Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy) — online only. AVOID: Sunrun.

tasks.title: Analyze energy usage and size system
tasks.notes: 17,178 kWh/yr at $0.42/kWh avg. Target: 5-7 panels south garage face (20x12 ft) = 2-2.8 kW. ~5yr payback after ITC.

tasks.title: Research and shortlist solar installers
tasks.notes: Shortlist: Sunergy (sunergy.com, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door), NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com, Diamond Certified, San Mateo County), Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy, online only). Avoid Sunrun.

tasks.title: Contact installers for quotes
tasks.notes: Spec: south garage face (20x12 ft, 5-7 panels, ~2-2.8 kW), paired battery (Enphase IQ or Powerwall 3), detached garage sub-panel interconnect. Ask each for: $/W, panel brand, inverter type, battery spec, warranty, timeline, ITC total. Request free shade/LIDAR analysis.

tasks.title: Compare quotes and select installer
tasks.notes: Prefer Enphase microinverters over string inverter. Compare $/W, battery spec, warranty, timeline. 30% ITC on full system incl. battery.

tasks.title: Confirm ITC timing — credit files in year PTO is granted
tasks.notes: 30% federal ITC on Form 5695. Claimed in tax year Permission to Operate granted by PG&E. Battery qualifies only when solar-paired. Plan install timing accordingly.

observations.summary: The audio appears to contain speech sounds recorded in the living room. — The rule guess suggests speech with a confidence of 0.75, supported by the mid-band energy concentration.

The audio contains intermittent, moderate-frequency sounds consistent with general outdoor activity, but no distinct event is identifiable. — The spectral centroid and mid-band energy suggest some activity, but the low energy variance and lack of strong transients make a specific classification difficult.

The audio appears to contain low-level, intermittent sounds consistent with general background noise in the living room. — The low energy levels and moderate spectral centroid suggest non-specific sounds, but the high mid-band energy points away from pure silence or pure speech/barking events.

The audio appears to contain low-level, intermittent background noise consistent with an outdoor setting, but no distinct sounds like barking or alarms were clearly detected. — The spectral centroid and mid-band energy suggest some activity, but the low energy variance and low overall RMS level make definitive classification difficult.

The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds consistent with potential alarms or sharp transients in the backyard. — The high spectral centroid (3159 Hz) and high high-band energy (0.582) suggest sharp, high-pitched sounds, but the low overall energy and low energy variance make a definitive classification difficult. The rule guess points to 'alarm' with 0.70 confidence.

The audio appears to contain dog barks, consistent with the presence of two Australian Shepherd dogs in the living room area. — The rule guess of 'bark' with a confidence of 0.72, combined with the high Mid-band energy (0.658) and the spectral characteristics, strongly suggests canine vocalizations.

The audio appears to contain low-level, intermittent background noise consistent with an outdoor setting, but no distinct sounds like barking or alarms are clearly present. — The spectral centroid and mid-band energy suggest some mid-range activity, but the low energy variance and low overall RMS level make definitive classification difficult.

The audio contains moderate energy across mid and high frequencies, but no distinct pattern matches the known sounds of the environment. — The spectral centroid is moderate, and the high-band energy is relatively high, but the overall energy variance is low, suggesting sustained, non-specific noise.

The audio appears to contain human speech recorded in the living room. — The rule guess strongly suggests speech, supported by the mid-band energy concentration and spectral centroid within the typical speech range.

The audio appears to contain intermittent bursts of sound consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy (0.502) and the peak/RMS ratio (3.80) suggest transient, non-continuous sounds, which aligns well with barking, especially given the presence of two dogs in the dog run.

The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds that are not clearly identifiable as a specific event like a bark or alarm in the backyard setting. — The high spectral centroid and high high-band energy suggest a sharp, possibly electronic or impact-related sound, but the overall pattern is too diffuse for a confident classification among the given options.

The audio appears to contain speech sounds recorded in the living room. — The rule guess strongly suggests speech, supported by the mid-band energy and spectral centroid, which are typical for human vocalizations.
LEAK DETECTED. Flow: 1.67 gal/min. Daily total: 407.8 gal.

The audio appears to contain intermittent bursts of sound consistent with dogs barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy (0.493) and the visual pattern of sharp, transient energy bursts in the spectrogram strongly suggest barking, which is expected given the presence of two Australian Shepherd dogs.
12 mobile devices home.

The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds consistent with an alarm or sharp transient event in the backyard. — The high spectral centroid (4135 Hz) and high high-band energy (0.709) suggest a sharp, high-pitched sound, but the overall pattern is not definitively characteristic of a known event like a bark or alarm trigger.

A distinct, sharp transient sound, consistent with a doorbell ring, was heard at the entryway. — The high mid-band energy, high peak/RMS ratio, and the visual evidence of sharp, periodic bursts in the spectrogram strongly suggest a doorbell ring, which is common at the entryway.

The front yard camera is currently failing to provide a video stream, displaying an error message. While audio indicates dogs are barking in the backyard, the visual view of the driveway and street is unavailable.

insights.summary: [front_yard] The front yard camera is currently failing to provide a video stream, displaying an error message instead.
[front_yard] The front yard camera is not transmitting video, preventing visual monitoring of the property.
[living_room] The dogs are barking, which may indicate they are reacting to something outside.

The period of high alert, characterized by alarm sounds, intense dog barking, and high-frequency noises, has significantly de-escalated. By 15:48, the environment is quiet, the front door is clear, and all family members are accounted for, suggesting the initial disturbance has passed and the household is settling into a normal late-afternoon routine.
